coredata.dc:title: Colour-tunable ultralong-lifetime room temperature phosphorescence with external heavy-atom effect in boron-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Qi
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Color-tunable ultralong-lifetime room temperature phosphorescence (URTP) materials have become powerful candidates for the extensive applications of optoelectronics, information encryption, anti-counterfeiting and sensing. Nevertheless, it hard to prepare URTP luminophores with both long afterglow lifetime and high phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) under ambient conditions. Herein, we developed a convenient, rapid and gram-scale method to successfully obtain a series of URTP boron-doped carbon dots (BDs, named as BD50, BD100, BD150 and BD300, respectively), utilizing boric acid (BA, 3 g) and citric acid (CA, 50, 100, 150 and 300 mg) as precursers. BD50 shows prominent multi-colour emissive photoluminescence (PL) and phosphorescence, with extraordinarily high PL quantum yield (PLQY, 40.4%) and PQY (23.5%). Moreover, BD50 exhibits bright yellow-green afterglow, with lifetime as high as 1.17 s. What worth more to joy and encourage is that significant improvements in PQY and lifetime were achieved by introducing halide ions into BDs, due to external heavy-atom effect (EHE). BD-X (X = Cl, Br and I) possesses high PQY and long lifetime (up to 34.4% and 2.61 s). Based on the unique RTP performance of BDs and BD-X, these URTP luminophores are successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. This work contributes to develop smart materials with ultralong afterglow and high phosphorescence efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron-doped carbon dots
Color-tunable
Ultralong-lifetime
Room temperature phosphorescence
External heavy-atom effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of BD50. (b) Size distributation of BD50. (c) Full scan XPS spectrum of BD50. HR XPS C 1 s (d) and B 1 s (e) spectra of BD50. (f) XRD pattern of BD50.

(a) UV–Vis absorption (black and red solid line) and emission spectra of BD50 aqueous solution. (b) UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of BD50 solid. (c) Phosphorescence excitation (PP Ex, black solid line) and emission spectra of BD50. (d) CIE coordinate diagram of BD50 under the excitation from 250 to 460 nm. (e) Photographs of BD50 with 254 nm UV lamp on and off. (f) Photographs of BD50 with 365 nm UV lamp on and off. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Afterglow emission spectra of (a) BD100, (b) BD150 and (c) BD300. (e) Digital photographs of BD100, BD150 and BD300 before and after ceasing the UV lamp (254 nm, d) and (365 nm, e), respectively.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and phosphorescence spectra of BD-Cl. (b) CIE coordinate diagram of BD-Cl under the excitation from 240 to 440 nm. RTP emission spectra of BD-Br (c) and BD-I (d). Photographs of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I with UV lamp (254 nm, e; 365 nm, f) on and off. RTP lifetime decay of BD-X under the excitation of 254 (g) and 365 nm (h).
a) Triplemodal switching application of BD50. b) Data encryption application of dry and wet BD50. c) Data encryption application of BD300, BD50, BD-I and BD-Cl.
Synthesis and applications of BDs (a) and BD-X (b).

head.ce:title: Colour-tunable ultralong-lifetime room temperature phosphorescence with external heavy-atom effect in boron-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yan an Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yan an Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Color-tunable ultralong-lifetime room temperature phosphorescence (URTP) materials have become powerful candidates for the extensive applications of optoelectronics, information encryption, anti-counterfeiting and sensing. Nevertheless, it hard to prepare URTP luminophores with both long afterglow lifetime and high phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) under ambient conditions. Herein, we developed a convenient, rapid and gram-scale method to successfully obtain a series of URTP boron-doped carbon dots (BDs, named as BD50, BD100, BD150 and BD300, respectively), utilizing boric acid (BA, 3 g) and citric acid (CA, 50, 100, 150 and 300 mg) as precursers. BD50 shows prominent multi-colour emissive photoluminescence (PL) and phosphorescence, with extraordinarily high PL quantum yield (PLQY, 40.4%) and PQY (23.5%). Moreover, BD50 exhibits bright yellow-green afterglow, with lifetime as high as 1.17 s. What worth more to joy and encourage is that significant improvements in PQY and lifetime were achieved by introducing halide ions into BDs, due to external heavy-atom effect (EHE). BD-X (X = Cl, Br and I) possesses high PQY and long lifetime (up to 34.4% and 2.61 s). Based on the unique RTP performance of BDs and BD-X, these URTP luminophores are successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. This work contributes to develop smart materials with ultralong afterglow and high phosphorescence efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have aroused increasing interest because of their great potential in photoelectricity, sensing, imaging, and safety encryption. Heretofore, RTP luminophores are restricted to metal-containing materials[1–4] and metal-free materials.[5–13] However, most RTP materials have short afterglow lifetimes (μs to ms)[14,15] and such rapid decays are inconvenient for their practical applications, because people usually need tens of milliseconds to identify afterglow emission.Scheme 1.

Recently, ultralong-lifetime RTP (URTP) materials have attracted wide attention, whose ultralong-lived excited states enable their phosphorescence to be captured by the naked eyes easily. Although great advances in persistent luminescence have been made by way of crystallization[16–20], H-aggregation[21–24], and embedding phosphors into various matrix[25–37], some phosphors exhibit long afterglow lifetime but low quantum efficiency[38], while some phosphors possess high phosphorescent quantum yield (PQY) but very short RTP lifetime[39]. To realize practical applications, it is critical to balance long lifetime and high quantum efficiency, due to the conflict of these two properties of RTP. Therefore, it remains a huge challenge to design URTP materials with high PQY and ultralong afterglow under ambient conditions.

Heavy-atom effect (HE) is known as one of the most popular ways to improve the performance of RTP luminogens[40], because it can promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) rate and efficiently enhance spin–orbit coupling (SOC). In general, HE is divided into two types: (1) Internal HE (IHE), by covalent binding of halogen atoms to the luminescent skeletons[41–45]; (2) External HE (EHE), by exploiting specific intermolecular interactions between external heavy atoms and luminophores[46–48]. Even though both IHE and EHE can facilitate phosphorescence, IHE is limited by the drawbacks of the high cost, and tedious preparation process. By contrast, EHE is regarded as a more simple and convenient strategy to achieve tunable RTP.

More prominently, color-tunable luminescent materials have promising applications in multicolor display, bioimaging, information encryption and anti-counterfeiting[49]. Although multicolor emissive materials can be prepared via various methods, most of them are fluorescence materials, rare RTP luminophores with color tunability have been explored so far.

Herein, we develop a simple and economical strategy to successfully synthesize URTP materials (boron-doped carbon dots, BDs) via pyrolysis method, using citric acid (CA) and boric acid (BA) as starting materials. The aqueous solution of BA (3 g) and CA (50, 100, 150 and 300 mg) was pyrolyzed at 180 °C to obtain BDs named BD50, BD100, BD150 and BD300, respectively. BD50 exhibits remarkable colour-tunable photoluminescence (PL) and phosphorescence, and its absolute PL quantum yield (PLQY) and phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) reaches up to 40.4% and 23.5%, respectively, which is among the best results for RTP nanomaterials reported heretofore. In addition, BD50 presents URTP lifetime of 1.17 s and bright yellow-green RTP is visible to naked eyes for 7 s with ceasing off 365 nm irradiation. Inspire by this, we try to further improve the PQY and afterglow lifetime of BDs by way of EHE. BD-X were prepared from BA (3 g), CA (50 mg) and KX (X = Cl, Br and I, 50 mg). Under 254 nm excitation, the PQY of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I is 7.4%, 7.8% and 11.3%, respectively, which is higher than that of BD50 (7.3%). More importantly, the RTP lifetime of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I is as high as 2.61, 2.52 and 2.32 s, which is in the highest level among RTP materials and the visible time of afterglow is as long as 12 s. Meanwhile, under 365 nm excitation, all of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I exhibit enhanced PQY and RTP lifetime, compared to BD50. EHE plays an essential role in elevating PQY and afterglow. Due to the excellent URTP performance of BDs and BD-X, they are successfully applied for time-resolved anti-counterfeiting and information protection. Last but not least, as mentioned above, BDs and BD-X can be facilely obtained from cheap raw materials in gram-scale, which is an intelligent strategy to construct URTP materials with high-efficiency and long afterglow lifetime.

BD50 was prepared by heating the aqueous solution of BA (3 g) and CA (50 mg) at 180 °C for 5 h (see supporting information). The morphological structure of BD50 was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). BD50 shows spherical shape (Fig. 1 a) and the average diameter is 31.9 ± 8.4 nm (Fig. 1b). The Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR, Figure S1) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were carried out to determine the surface groups and chemical compositions of BD50. The distinct absorption band at 3230 cm−1 and 1721 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of –OH and C = O. The bands at 1458 cm−1 and 1196 cm−1 are attributed to B-O and B-O–H, respectively. The characteristic bands of B-O-C and B-C are at 1046 cm−1 and 952 cm−1, respectively The full-scan XPS spectrum of BD50 (Fig. 1c) displays the presence of O, C and B with atomic percentages of 57.8%, 32.8% and 9.4%. The characteristic peak of O1s, C1s and B1s is at 531.5, 283.2 and 192.7 eV, respectively. The high resolution (HR) XPS spectrum of C1s exhibits three components of C–C/C = C (284.6 eV), C-O (285.6 eV) and C = O (287.8 eV) (Fig. 1d). The three peaks at 192.8 eV, 193.5 eV and 194.4 eV in HR B1s XPS spectrum are attributed to BCO2, B2O3 and B-O, respectively (Fig. 1e)[50,51]. XPS analysis is fully consistent with FT-IR result. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of BD50 has typical peaks of B2O3 at 14.6°, 28.0°, 30.4° and 40.2° (Fig. 1f).

The photophysical properties of BD50 was characterized in detail. Two peaks at 218 and 336 nm are observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of BD50 aqueous solution (Fig. 2 a, black solid line and red solid line). The PL spectra of BD50 aqueous solution presents excitation-dependent feature, with the maximum emission at 430 nm under 340 nm excitation. However, no afterglow was observed from BD50 solution due to the molecular rotation, vibration, and collisional triplet relaxation. The solid-state BD50 have three absorption bands at 225, 298 and 361 nm (Fig. 2b, black solid line). The former one is ascribed to the π - π* transition of C = C, while the latter two arise from the n - π* transition of C = O. The PL excitation spectrum of BD50 has one peak at 372 nm (Fig. 2b, red solid line), which is in conformity to its absorption band at 300–400 nm, conforming that PL emission mainly comes from C = O bonds of BD50. Both fluorescence (Fig. 2b) and phosphorescence (Fig. 2c) emission of BD50 manifest excitation dependency, with the maximum emission at 430 nm and 496 nm, respectively. Fig. 2c indicates that the phosphorescence emission of BD50 mainly shifts gradually from 476 nm to 532 nm, with increasing excitation wavelength. The Commission International del' Eclairage (CIE) coordinates were calculated to demonstrate the phosphorescence emission color variations of BD50 upon excitation wavelength. The colour-tunable phosphorescence of BD50 is depicted in Fig. 2d, BD50 changes from blue-green to yellow-green, upon the excitation shifting from 250 to 460 nm. The absolute PL quantum yield (PLQY) of BD50 reaches up to 40.4%, which is one of the best results for RTP nanomaterials heretofore. The absolute phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) of BD50 is as high as 23.5% (Table S2), which is in the highest level among organic nonmetallic RTP nanomaterials. BD50 emits blue light under irradiation of 254 nm UV lamp and presents yellow-green afterglow with the lamp off (Fig. 2e). In addition, BD50 can exhibit blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation, and ultralong-lived yellow-green RTP was observed after the removal of excitation source and could last up to 7 s (Fig. 2f). The phosphorescence decay curve of BD50 at 365 nm excitation (Figure S5) can be fitted with a triexponential function and the average lifetime was calculated to be 1.17 s (Table S2). The results demonstrate that the existence of mutiple triple-excitated states might result in the excitation dependent feature of BD50. The PL and phosphorescence emission spectra of BD50 solid are totally different, verifying the RTP nature of afterglow. Furthermore, the disappearance of afterglow in the solution indicates that the solid state can stabilize the performance of RTP.

Some control experiments were conducted to obtain a further grasp of the activation of RTP. No RTP was observed when CA or BA was used alone as a precursor. Then, 3 g of BA reacted with different mass (100, 150 and 300 mg) of CA under a similar condition to obtain BDs named BD100, BD150 and BD300, respectively. The absorption spectra (Figure S3) of BD100, BD150 and BD300 are similar with that of BD50, which have three feature peaks at 224, 299 and 356 nm. All of BD100, BD150 and BD300 exhibit excitation dependent PL behaviors with the emission maxima at 442, 438 and 487 nm, respectively, under the optimal excitation of 380, 380 and 400 nm (Figure S4). Under the excitation of UV lamp of 254 nm and 365 nm, BD100, BD150 and BD300 emit greenish-blue light (Fig. 3 d and 3e). Moreover, BD100, BD150 and BD300 exhibit obviously excitation-dependent RTP features, and their phosphorescence optimal emission is recorded at 517, 525 and 526 nm, respectively (Fig. 3a–c). When the UV lamp of 254 nm is off, BD100, BD150 and BD300 show very faint green RTP, which is visible only for 2 s (Fig. 3d). However, as ceasing UV lamp of 365 nm, BD100 and BD150 display intense yellow-green and yellow RTP, up to 6 s by naked eyes, respectively (Fig. 3e). BD300 exhibits yellow RTP, which is visible to naked eyes for 4 s (Fig. 3e). The absolute PLQY of BD50, BD100, BD150 and BD300 graduallylowers from 40.4%, 33.3%, 29.6% to 22.5%. The absolute PQY of BD50, BD100, BD150 and BD300 decreases from 23.5%, 20.7%, 17.4% to 11.3% (Table S2). The average lifetime (Figure S5, Table S2) of BD100, BD150 and BD300 is 1.24, 1.13 and 0.98 s, respectively. Based on the results mentioned above, BD50 exhibits the best fluorescence and phosphorescence characteristics.

The main characteristic peaks in the XRD patterns of BD100, BD150 and BD300 are similar with that of BD50 (Figure S6). The chemical compositions of BD100, BD150 and BD300 were also investigated by XPS (Figure S7-S9). The B content (Table S3) in BD50, BD100, BD150 and BD300 shows a trend of gradual decline from 12.7%, 8.2%, 7.5% to 5.1%. The B-C and B2O3 contents, as estimated by HR XPS spectrum of B 1 s, are listed in Table S4. The B-C content in BD50, BD100 and BD150 gradually reduces. On the contrary, lots of B2O3 appears at the high content of 78.2%. These results indicate that the formed B-C covalent bonds between CA and BA could improve the RTP properties of BDs. FTIR spectra of BD100, BD150 and BD300 are shown in Figure S1, which resemble that of BD50.

Based on the discussion mentioned above, we speculate on a possible mechanism of BDs phosphorescence. Firstly, the C = O bonds can induce RTP of BDs. Secondly, BA is also vital for the production of RTP, which can react with CA to form B-C bonds. The covalent bonds and nanoconfined space of BA could hinder the excited triplet excitons from extinguishing, thereby facilitating RTP emission. The energy gaps (ΔEST) between the lowest singlet state (S1) and the triplet state (T1) were deduced by the PL and phosphorescence emission spectra of BDs and listed in Table S5, all of which are small values (0.31–0.49 eV) for effective ISC process.

It is well known that EHE can trigger significant spin–orbit coupling (SOC) phenomenon and greatly promotes RTP performance. Inspired by this, KCl, KBr, KI was introduced into BD50 to prepare BD-X (X = Cl, Br, I), respectively, in order to achieve efficient phosphorescence and enhance RTP. The absorption spectra of BD-X (Fig. 4 a and Figure S10a) are similar with that of BD50. BD-X still maintains excitation-dependent RTP (Fig. 4a-e) and PL emission (Figure S11). BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I present blue, blue-green and yellow fluorescence under 254 nm UV lamp, respectively. To our delight, when the lamp is switched off, URTP appears with visually recognizable period of more than 12 s (Fig. 4e). A video documenting the luminescence of BD50, BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I before and after ceasing UV lamp (254 nm) was depicted in video S1. Furthermore, BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I show remarkably long lifetime of 2.61, 2.52 and 2.32 s, respectively (Fig. 4g, Table S1), which is very rare for organic nonmetal RTP luminophors so far. Moreover, it is much longer than that of BD50 (1.10 s). The ΔEST value of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I was determined to be as low as 0.15, 0.15 and 0.27 eV (Table S6), which is very favorable for effective ISC and SOC processes. BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I have blue, blue-green and yellow emission under 365 nm irradiation, respectively. After the removal of lamp (Fig. 4f), BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I exhibit green-yellow, yellow-green and yellow RTP, and the ΔEST was calculated to be 0.35, 0.35 and 0.39 eV (Table S5). The RTP lifetime of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I is 1.21, 1.27 and 1.34 s, respectively, all of them are longer that that of BD50 (1.17 s). Video S2 shows the luminescence of BD50, BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I with UV lamp (365 nm) on and off. Moreover, the PQY of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I is 28.8%, 34.4% and 24.9%, which is also higher than that of BD50. On the other hand, The FTIR spectra (Figure S10b) and XRD (Figure S10c) of BD-Cl, BD-Br and BD-I resemble those of BD50. These results confirm that the introduction of halogen ions generates EHE, which can boost the effective phosphorescence and realize tunable RTP.

Thanks to the extraordinary URTP property of BDs and BD-X, they have great potential in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. Firstly, BD50 was utilized as a model to demonstrate their promising applications as smart materials in security protection. Fig. 5 a depicts a distinctive triplemodal switching encryption by loading BD50 powder into a panda mould. Under the daylight, the panda pattern is white. Upon 365 nm irradiation, the panda pattern displays bright blue fluorescence. Once turning off light, intense yellow-green RTP from the panda pattern presents. In addition, since the phosphorescence emission of BD50 can be quenched by water, which could be applied in anti-counterfeiting. As displayed in Fig. 5b, BD50 was used to spell out the letters CCUT, then water was sprayed to the second letter C and the fourth letter T. In daylight, there is no difference between the four letters. Upon 365 nm irradiation, the first letter C and the third letter T glow bright blue light. By contrast, the second letter C and the fourth letter T emit very faint blue fluorescence. When the UV light is switched off, only the first letter C and the third letter T can be recognized as lightful yellow-green phosphorescence. In addition, making use of the distinctive RTP performance of BDs and BD-X, we carried out time-resolved RTP anti-counterfeiting. As shown in Fig. 5c, BD300, BD50, BD-I and BD-Cl were loaded into the letter patterns of CCUT, respectively. The letters CCUT are visible under 254 nm irradiation. When 254 nm UV light is switched off, because BD300, BD50, BD-I and BD-Cl have different RTP lifetimes, the letters CCU disappear at 1, 4 and 9 s, respectively. Finally, only the letter T can be seen with naked eyes. The above results demonstrate that BDs and BD-X can be practically applicable for advanced anti-counterfeiting and information protection.

We provid a type of straightforward and economical method to obtain color-tunable URTP BDs. BD50 solid exhibit high PLQY (40.4%) and PQY (23.5%) with 365 nm excitation on and off, respectively. Furthermore, BD50 possesses ultralong RTP lifetime of 1.17 s with the visible time of afterglow for 6 s. Even more noteworthy is that we developed a distinct strategy to modulate RTP performance by incorporating halide anions (Cl−, Br−, and I−). The as-prepared BD-X exhibit improved phosphorescence efficiency with highest PQY of 34.4% and RTP lifetime of 2.61 s. Moreover, BDs and BD-X are successfully employed for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. This work offers a feasible and highly effective way to obtain a new kind of URTP luminophors from cheap raw materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023 and 51973214) and the Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Project (No. 20200201088JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.127647.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Liquid crystalline copper(i) complexes with bright room temperature phosphorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Giménez, O. Crespo, B. Diosdado, A. Elduque, Liquid crystalline copper(i) complexes with bright room temperature phosphorescence, J. Mater. Chem. C 8 (2020) 6552-6557.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo-stimulated polychromatic room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Hu, Y. Wang, Z. Li, H. Lin, Photo-stimulated polychromatic room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots, Small 16 (2020) e2001909.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, Y. Wang, C. Cai, H. Lin, Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications, Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1800783.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile, quick, and gram-scale synthesis of ultralong-lifetime room-temperature-phosphorescent carbon dots by microwave irradiation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, Y. Wang, X. Gao, C. Cai, H. Lin, Facile, quick, and gram-scale synthesis of ultralong-lifetime room-temperature-phosphorescent carbon dots by microwave irradiation, Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 57 (2018) 6216-6220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbonized polymer dots with tunable room-temperature phosphorescence lifetime and wavelength

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Xia, S. Zhu, S.-T. Zhang, Q. Zeng, S. Tao, X. Tian, Y. Li, B. Yang, Carbonized polymer dots with tunable room-temperature phosphorescence lifetime and wavelength, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2020, 12, 38593−38601 .

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White light emission from a single organic molecule with dual phosphorescence at room temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. He, W. Zhao, J.W.Y. Lam, Q. Peng, H. Ma, G. Liang, Z. Shuai, B.Z. Tang, White light emission from a single organic molecule with dual phosphorescence at room temperature, Nat. Commun. 8 (2017) 416.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-protective room-temperature phosphorescence of fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Long, Y. Feng, C. Cao, Y. Li, J. Han, S. Li, C. Peng, Z. Li, W. Feng, Self-protective room-temperature phosphorescence of fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots, Adv. Funct. Mater. 28 (2018) 1800791.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Matrix-free and highly efficient room-temperature phosphorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Gao, H. Han, W. Lu, Y. Jiao, Y. Liu, X. Gong, M. Xian, S. Shuang, C. Dong, Matrix-free and highly efficient room-temperature phosphorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots, Langmuir 34 (2018) 12845-12852.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Frontiers in carbon dots: design, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, L. Wang, Y. Li, Y. Feng, W. Feng, Frontiers in carbon dots: design, properties and applications. Mater. Chem. Front. 3, (2019) 2571-2601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: self-protective room-temperature phosphorescence of fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Long, Y. Feng, C. Cao, Y. Li, J. Han, S. Li, C. Peng, Z. Li, W. Feng, Carbon dots: self-protective room-temperature phosphorescence of fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots. Adv. Funct. Mater, 28, (2018) 1870263.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room temperature phosphorescence lifetime and spectrum tuning of substituted thianthrenes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Pander, A. Swist, J. Soloducho, F.B. Dias, Room temperature phosphorescence lifetime and spectrum tuning of substituted thianthrenes, Dyes Pigm. 142 (2017) 315-322.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-afterglow metal–organic frameworks: reversible guest-induced phosphorescence tunability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, D. Yan, Long-afterglow metal–organic frameworks: reversible guest-induced phosphorescence tunability, Chem. Sci. 7 (2016) 4519-4526.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crystal engineering of room temperature phosphorescence in organic solids

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic nanocrystals with bright red persistent room-temperature phosphorescence for biological applications
